title: From Edges to Objects
concept: CNNs build a feature hierarchy: early layers notice simple edges, middle layers combine them into corners, textures, and parts, and deeper layers assemble those parts into whole-object clues.

exercises.prompt: Recap: If an early CNN layer can spot simple edges, what is a later layer most useful for doing with those edge detections?

options.text: Combining them into larger patterns like corners, textures, or object parts
Turning every edge back into the original RGB pixels
Deleting all spatial structure so the image becomes random noise

exercises.explain: Exactly. CNNs do not stop at edges — they keep stacking simple detections into bigger and bigger visual ideas.

cards.title: A cat doesn't appear all at once
cards.body: When a CNN recognizes a cat, it usually does not jump straight from raw pixels to cat in one magical leap. It climbs a ladder: tiny edges first, then little shapes, then ears, whiskers, and eyes, and only then a strong cat-level pattern.

cards.title: What a feature hierarchy is
cards.body: A feature hierarchy means different depths of a CNN specialize in different levels of complexity. Early layers tend to detect simple patterns like edges. Middle layers combine those into corners, textures, and repeated motifs. Deeper layers combine those again into parts and whole-object clues.

cards.title: How an artist sketches
cards.body: A drawing often starts with short pencil strokes. Those strokes become outlines. The outlines become shapes like a head or a hand. The finished sketch emerges from many little marks combined in the right order. A CNN builds meaning in that same step-by-step way.

cards.title: One dog photo, four levels
cards.body: Imagine a dog photo. Layer 1 responds to short bright-dark edges. Layer 2 notices things like corner junctions and fluffy textures. Layer 3 combines those into dog-part clues like an ear triangle or nose bridge. Layer 4 may light up strongly when several of those parts appear in a dog-like arrangement.

cards.title: Deep layers are not starting from scratch
cards.body: A common myth is that a deep layer somehow looks at pixels all by itself and directly invents an object detector. Not really. Later layers mostly read the feature maps made by earlier layers, so their strength comes from building on top of earlier visual clues.

cards.title: Early vs. Deep Features
cards.body: Same image, but different layers care about different kinds of structure:

right.text:
Parts and arrangements
like eyes, wheels, or ears
built from earlier clues

exercises.prompt: Which sequence best matches the usual CNN feature hierarchy?

options.text: Edges → random noise → empty maps → objects

exercises.explain: Right — CNNs usually move from simple local patterns to richer and more global ones as depth increases.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Later CNN layers often use earlier feature detections as ingredients for more complex detections.
exercises.explain: True — that ingredient-stacking idea is the whole reason we call it a hierarchy.

exercises.sentence: In a feature hierarchy, deeper layers often combine simple edge patterns into object ___ .
exercises.explain: Parts is right — things like ears, wheels, or windows are common middle-to-deep layer building blocks.

exercises.prompt: Which statement best explains why CNN depth matters for object recognition?

options.text: Because each deeper stage can reuse simpler earlier detections and assemble them into more meaningful structures
Because deeper layers stop using patterns and only memorize file names
Because early and deep layers always detect exactly the same thing

exercises.explain: Exactly — the whole stack works like a builder, not a single one-shot detector.

exercises.prompt: A vision model sees a bicycle photo. Its early layers fire on short edges, middle layers fire on curved rim pieces and spoke textures, and a deeper layer fires when wheel-like and handlebar-like parts appear together. What does this pattern show?

options.text: The CNN is using a feature hierarchy, building from simple local clues up to object-level structure
The network is broken, because all layers should detect whole bicycles immediately
The deeper layer must be reading only raw pixels and ignoring earlier layers
The feature maps are actually storing the bike's file format instead of visual content

exercises.explain: Exactly. That's the ladder in action: simple patterns become parts, and parts become object clues. Next: how deeper layers compose many small features into larger ones.
